speaker: interviewer
text: --with Paula Lebovics. Today is March 16, 1995. I am Donna Kanter, the interviewer. The kitchens where you would get-- you were speaking of getting the potato.

speaker: interviewee
text: Peels.

speaker: interviewer
text: Peels.

speaker: interviewee
text: There-- I was-- when-- if you look at it from Block One, beyond Block One, there were all the utility kind of barracks, I guess. And one of them must have been the kitchen because it was ahead of-- we were seven. And then there would be five, and three, and one, whatever. And then there would have probably been the kitchen.

speaker: interviewer
text: So around that space, there were all kinds of storerooms. But at that particular time, when we were there already, as I try to think about it and say it, our camp was not an ordinary kind that you could see people-- like, even in the [GERMAN], maybe some blocks would get up and go to work. But here, nobody got up anywhere, even from anywhere. It was just-- we were the children's block.

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember there was one block that was-- nuns were there. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And on the odd side of the street, which was either one, three, or five. I don't remember which one. And-- and then I found out about the hospital. So the hospital was there. And then later on, I found out that there was like-- if you can call it that-- an old-age block, which people that couldn't move or were-- were-- were somehow, they got into the camp, even though probably the Germans didn't want them. But through weeding out, maybe some people, like us children, got somehow in there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was getting close to the end-- end of-- of the war. And we started to hear all kinds of bombings around us. And winter was approaching. And we were-- we were still in that block.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, what happened is towards the end, I remember, and my friend told me that this man that came in, with a few others with him, was Mengele. Because I never remember seeing him. Because like I say, I was so oblivious. And I had my own little world. But my friend told me this was Mengele. There were three of us from the same camp, and towards the end, we were all three together all the time. We slept on the same bank. Nobody told us if we can't sleep there or we can. We didn't really have a head of the-- of the barracks, so to speak. It was-- it was like, free-for-all type, except for the Germans. They still came in all the time. We still did not have any peace at all, ever. But it seemed to have been a little more relaxed type.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I remember one time, they came in. And that was towards the very end. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I was told this was Mengele that came in. He came in. And he tried to make us relax.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he asked us to please form like a circle around him, all around him. And all the kids just went around. And we stood around there. You didn't dare to disobey a German ever, ever. And we stood around there. And he's telling us that-- I don't remember whether he told us that we are good. We are good, and we are this. And he tried to sort of butter us up. And then he said, I know that a lot-- all of you have-- have family that are not with you. And if you want to be reunited with your family, we can do it for you. And all you have to do is step into the middle. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he gave us this speech. And I stepped in the middle. And I-- during those years in the camp, I had a little girl that was from our hometown. And I-- during that last part, she attached herself to me. She was younger than me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she attached herself to me. And no matter what I did, she did. So when I stepped in the middle, she stepped in the middle. And all of a sudden, my-- my light bulb went up in my head, and I said, how do they know who my family is? And if I tell them, maybe I jeopardize-- and this all went up in my head in-- in a matter of seconds. I'm talking about seconds, not minutes. And I-- as I stepped in, almost simultaneously, she stepped in. And almost simultaneously after she stepped in, I stepped back. I mean, it was like, dum, bum, bum, because a light bulb went up in my head saying, how do they know who my family is? And if I-- if I do this, no, it's not good. That's preservation.

speaker: interviewee
text: I stepped back. And I tried to make her see to step back too. But she wouldn't. She just stood there. She just stood there. And he kept on talking out. And quite a few of the children stepped in. And they took them away. Well, we later found their bodies right outside after liberation. And don't forget, this was winter. And their bodies were intact. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They were right outside-- out-- out-- outside the camps. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Shot?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they were shot. They were shot. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This was towards the end. And like I say, the bombing went on all the time after that. And I remember being-- we specially went up, the three of us-- my friends from our hometown. They are in the pictures too, from Auschwitz. And they-- we-- we laid on the top. And we prayed for those bombs to come so badly. We wanted-- we knew that this was meant-- that we're going to be out of here. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] about 10 days before liberation, I would say, maybe a day or two, or maybe it was before then that-- that the bombs fell and knocked out-- knocked out the electricity of the camp, so there was no more electric. I think it might have been right after the last patrol. Now, about 10 days before liberation, the German patrol [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] took away the last [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] people, because they were taken away. My brother was gone. I mean, this was-- all through January, the people were just marching out, and it was less and less people. And the whole camp next to us, my brother's camp, was completely empty. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And in our camp, even, they were emptying out. But about 10 days before liberation, the last patrol, I remember they came on motorcycles, like with an attached little wagon type. And they pulled up in front of our block. And I don't remember whether they took some of our children or not. They might have, because some of them were bigger than others. They might have taken some. But they didn't take me. They-- they left us. And-- and they went to the hospital, evidently, because I found it out after liberation. And they took my mother. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they marched them away. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] After they went, we had no Germans, nothing. We were all free. We were by ourselves. There was no Russians, no Germans, no nothing-- no electricity, no nothing. First thing we all did is go into the storeroom and got bread. And we-- I remember holding my bread under my, whatever I was wearing. I had bread, bread. Wherever I-- wherever you touched me, I had bread. I was completely insulated well. And the second thing we did is go into the storerooms and got ourselves dressed warmly. So if you see on those pictures all of us wearing babushkas and nice clothes, it's because we went to the storerooms, and we got ourselves dressed after the Germans left. Because this is not the kind of clothes we were liberated-- I mean, these are not the kind of clothes that we were in the camp with. This-- this was heaven. We dressed ourselves. And I remember having this particular cute story is that I wanted to have boots. I wanted to have boots, and I couldn't find boots that were matching. And they had to be boots.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I found one boot in-- in like a luggage color, a deep luggage color, like a brownish luggage color. It was brownish leather with-- with that caca-color [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] felt. But I couldn't find the mate. That fit me a little better. I couldn't find a mate.

speaker: interviewee
text: I finally was so frustrated-- and they-- all the kids, they wanted to leave-- that I-- I found another boot, and it was black leather with white felt. Close enough. So I had-- [LAUGHS] and it was much bigger.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was-- it's too bad that the pictures cannot show this, because I remember there was nothing happier in my life than finding these boots at that time and putting on warm-- warm shoes. But little did I realize-- I went outside in that snow, and they soaked right through. [LAUGHS] But it was heaven. We all were dressed nicely.

speaker: interviewee
text: And on the 27th of January, the Russians came in. And they liberated us. A lot of people had-- have bad memories from that. But I have good ones. I'm very grateful. As-- as I was relating it to [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Petrenk--

speaker: interviewee
text: To Petrenko, when he came to visit Los Angeles, that I had a particular wonderful story about a soldier picking me up, sitting down, and rocking me in his arms. And the tears were just flowing down his face. And I can never forget that as long as I live. Just to look at him and figuring in my head, you mean, there is somebody-- like, somebody out there cares about me? It was-- It was the first time I had this kind of feeling. I thought only your mother or somebody. But somebody caring about me, it was overwhelming. He wanted to share his food. And he had slonina, which is like slab bacon. And I showed him my stash of bread. And I said, no, that's all I want is the bread. Look how much I have. But I did take a little piece of his black bread. He had that Russian black bread. But they didn't have very much. The soldiers didn't look like the-- the Germans, all dressed up. They-- they looked kind of tattered and worn and beaten up. They did not look the same, like the Germans did, with the-- with the clanking of their boots. They didn't hardly had anything like that. And this-- this is my recollection of the Russian liberation. And after that, [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I don't know how-- if it was days or nothing. They dressed us up in those striped uniforms, which we never had before, ever, because those were warm. And they took pictures. And I was lucky enough-- I attached myself to one Russian soldier. And I went with them on the Jeep when they were filming. Now, the films that you see at the liberation of Auschwitz are unreal. And no human being can explain it, what it looked like. I'm sure you've seen the pictures. I'm sure you saw real pictures that they show. Well, this is what we were liberated with. There were bodies everywhere that died afterwards, and those were gathered up in front of each camp and put in big piles. And we were lucky it was winter, so the bodies stayed intact. And they were not causing any health hazards. That's what we found out later. Of course, I was a child. I didn't know all this stuff. All I know is that when I went around with that Russian, and they were filming the devastation of Auschwitz with those mass graves and all these things that you only see on film, this is what we had in Auschwitz. And afterwards, they-- then they actually transferred us to Auschwitz, to Auschwitz, which was different. There were not barracks. There were regular, like, army barracks, which were not made out of wood. They were real barracks made out of something or another, but not wood. And that's where we were concentrated until-- oh, yeah, a very important thing happened. After a couple of days, somebody told me that they found-- they saw my mother, one of our-- from our hometown, the children. And my mother and I were reunited, because she was taken away with that last transport. And that's how I found out that she was taken away. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: To where?

speaker: interviewee
text: --We ended up together in Auschwitz, in the blocks.

speaker: interviewer
text: She had been transported where?

speaker: interviewee
text: By that last German patrol, right outside of Auschwitz. And they left them there because the front was right there. So the soldiers either were taken captive, or they ran away or whatever. And all these people were just left there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they were a few kilometers out of Auschwitz when they were left there alone. So my mother and I were together, and we were in-- in the big blocks of Auschwitz. I don't know how long we stayed in Auschwitz. I don't remember. I really don't.

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember, after a while, we loaded up on a-- on a little cart. And we drove to Krakow with our belongings. Our belongings consisted of two-- those-- that-- that was more than gold and diamonds and everything else put together. We had two blankets. They were, like, rolled up. And I was in charge of the blankets.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my mother and I went to Krakow. And my mother found that she knows somebody in Krakow from our hometown. And she wanted to borrow some money or something, so that we can go back home to Ostrowiec.

speaker: interviewee
text: For us, the war was over. And we stayed in that big, like a community center in-- in Krakow, which was like-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I recognized that building in They had a film about children that were orphaned.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they couldn't find their parents, and they stayed there in Krakow, and they took them away. That was the building which I was in until we found a way to go back to Ostrowiec.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's where we stayed. And that's where the Red Cross [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] put our name on the list as survivors. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that's where-- where it was that an interviewer from the Red Cross tried to interview me about what I went through. And I remember, I started telling her. And when it came part to tell her that my brother was a policeman, I wouldn't divulge it. Because I was afraid because some of them were just horrid. And the people went after them and killed them after the war. They did.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when it came to that part, I ran out of the room screaming, and I never went back and never wanted to be interviewed. And you couldn't get me to talk for 20 years.

speaker: interviewee
text: I wouldn't tell. I-- I just was too scared to talk about that, even though my brother was very good. And he was only a-- a policeman for a little while. Because when we came to the camp, he was no longer a policeman.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to the rest of your family? You reunited with some, and others, no.

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody was liberated. This-- the war wasn't over when we were liberated. But we managed to find some people in Krakow from our hometown who had a-- a better fate than we did. And Poland was already liberated, mostly. And they gave us some money. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My mother borrowed or something. I don't remember. I remember going to some people's houses and see the luxury they lived in. Here I come out of Auschwitz. I was devastated, devastated-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] --to find out that-- that there were some people that lived normal. Well, we got some money.

speaker: interviewer
text: [BACKGROUND NOISE] How did you find your way to the United States, and what happened to the rest of your family between the end of the war and coming here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Could you repeat it. I was not paying attention. I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: The telephone. I understand. How did you go back to your home and then work, eventually, your way to the United States, and with whom?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, like I say, from-- from Krakow, we-- we went on an open train. It must've been around-- it was in February. It had to be in February or March or whatever. I don't remember how long we stayed in Auschwitz. So it had to be in the winter still, or very late winter, or early spring, or whatever. We went back to Ostrowiec on those open trains. And like I say, our-- our big belongings were those two blankets. And I snoozed and losed-- lost the blankets. I fell asleep on that open train. It was like a-- it was like a commercial train-- open. I don't know what they used to do. It was like a cattle train, but it was open. I mean, there was no roof. And I must have fallen asleep, and somebody took them. And I remember my father yelling at me for it, that I lost them. I do remember that.

speaker: interviewer
text: After you got home, what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: We got back to Ostrowiec. So we went back to our building where-- my grandfather's building. And the caretaker was no longer living in the basement apartment. She was living on the second floor in the-- in the main building.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we asked her to give us a room, because a lot of other people came back with us too that were liberated. And we wanted one room so we can huddle, or we'd be together. So she says, [RUSSIAN], you Jew? You mean they didn't kill you?

speaker: interviewee
text: And she wouldn't give us a room. But finally, she did give us a room. She did give us a room in our own home. And I remember-- I don't remember how many people we were in that room. And then we got the second room too. So we had two rooms. I don't know how many of us were in those two rooms. But they were whoever came to our hometown. We somehow managed to sleep on the floor, or wherever it was. We-- we managed. And we stayed there [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] until the war was over. And when the war was over, apparently our names circulated everywhere because we were on the Red Cross list. So when my brother found our name, he was already-- he got liberated I don't know when. After lib-- maybe when it was after the war, or just before. I don't know. And he found our name. He was already in-- in Italy. And he was going to go to Israel. He was in Milano. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I remember because he wrote to us from Milano [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to tell us to wait for him. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he put his name on the list, naturally. And every day, we used to go to our-- there was one room in our hometown where they made it like the community room, and they posted all the things that came in that day. And they would post those lists, lists. There were-- and one day, we found the list with his name on it. And it-- it was wonderful. And then we got-- we received a letter from him to say to wait for us-- for him in our hometown. And we did.

speaker: interviewer
text: But once he got back to our hometown, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the Poles desecrated-- not only the Poles, but the Germans had desecrated the cemetery, naturally. So the whole group, whoever was liberated, tried to right it, right the cemetery, or at least to put some of the stones back up or something. And we had-- in our hometown, there was a very famous rabbi who was like a saint, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: He-- you know, in Europe, you follow a rabbi. And-- and he happened to live in our hometown. He was very famous, the [RUSSIAN]. He was very famous. And his grave was there. So they-- they stood up his grave, and we had a community kind of meeting. Right after that, I think almost within a couple of nights after that, the Poles came in in one of those rooms where people were huddling, and they killed 10 people. They just slaughtered them. And one of those 10 survived because he sort of-- I guess he either passed out or acted as dead. And he survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my brother said, we're packing our-- we're getting to higher ground. So we went to Lodz. And that's where we moved to Lodz. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And in Lodz, we were there until the Russians took over. I mean, when they came into power, my brother says-- well, he was so much older. He knew all the politics and everything. He says, we're not staying here. We're getting out of here.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in those days, people were making sort of a living exchanging money or whatever. We had nothing. Even the blankets were gone.

speaker: interviewee
text: We stayed with some people who managed to get an apartment. And like I said, my mother had borrowed money from these people in-- in-- in Krakow. So we stayed with these people in Lodz. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And my brother took me-- that he found out that they have a DP camp in West Germany. So I was the first one to be taken out of Poland. But when I got to Lodz, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was still early. It was right after-- practically after liberation, which was, let's say, July or something. And I had a chance to go to school in September, in Polish school in Lodz. And that's when I first started learning, really, reading and writing and everything else. And in three months I was there, I managed to skip three grades. I was very proud of myself. I was 12 years old. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And my brother took me across and tried to smuggle me across from Poland, through Eastern Poland, through Eastern Germany to West Germany. And we were caught on the border by the Russians-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] smuggling. And we ended up in a camp for two weeks. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But I was very lucky [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that they let us go and-- and stay in a camp. They didn't shoot us.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll pick up with your liberation with the next tape.
